diagrams.title: Defense in Depth Architecture
diagrams.description: A layered security model showing multiple concentric rings of protection around the core assets, from outermost perimeter security to innermost data protection. Each layer provides independent security controls.
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the defense in depth principle - that multiple layers of security controls provide better protection than a single perimeter, ensuring that if one layer fails, others still provide protection.

diagrams.title: Least Privilege vs. Excessive Access
diagrams.description: A comparison diagram showing two user scenarios - one with minimal necessary permissions (least privilege) and another with excessive access rights, highlighting the security risks of over-privileged accounts.
elements: User A icon with minimal permissions badge
User B icon with excessive permissions badge
Access pathway lines showing permission scope
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the least privilege principle - users should only have the minimum access necessary to perform their job functions, reducing the attack surface and potential damage from compromised accounts.

diagrams.title: Security Design Principles Overview
diagrams.description: A visual matrix or wheel diagram presenting the eight fundamental security design principles organized by category, showing their relationships and how they interconnect in secure system design.
diagrams.purpose: Provides a comprehensive overview of all key security design principles including least privilege, separation of duties, fail secure, economy of mechanism, complete mediation, open design, and least common mechanism, showing how they work together in security architecture.
